title: How to Spot Logical Fallacies in Your Own Essays
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You know what a straw man is. You can spot ad hominem attacks in political debates. But are you accidentally committing logical fallacies in your own essays? Probably. Here is how to edit your arguments like a logician, catching errors before your professor does.

The Problem with Passionate Writing

When you care about a topic, you want to convince your reader. That passion can lead to shortcuts. You make a leap in logic. You attack the opposing view unfairly. You assume because something happened before, it caused what came after. These are fallacies. They weaken your argument.

Good editing is not just about fixing commas. It's about fixing logic. Read your essay with a skeptical eye. Ask: would someone who disagrees with me find a flaw here?

The Straw Man (Most Common in Student Essays)

A straw man is when you misrepresent an opposing view to make it easier to attack. 'Opponents of school uniforms believe students should be able to wear anything, even offensive slogans.'

This is not what opponents believe. They believe in reasonable dress codes without uniforms. You've built a straw man. You knock it down. You feel victorious. But you haven't engaged with the real argument.

Fix: Describe the opposing view fairly, in a way your opponent would recognize. Then explain why you still disagree. This is harder. It's also more persuasive.

Ad Hominem (Attacking the Person)

'The senator proposed a climate bill, but he flies a private jet.' This attacks the person, not the policy. Even if the senator is hypocritical, the climate bill might still be good policy.

In your essays, avoid attacking the character of people you disagree with. Stick to their arguments. If you must mention character, make it part of a larger point about credibility, not a substitute for evidence.

Post Hoc Ergo Propter Hoc (False Cause)

'After the school implemented uniforms, test scores went up. Therefore, uniforms caused the increase.' This is a fallacy. Maybe something else changed. Maybe the test was easier. Maybe the scores were going up anyway.

Correlation is not causation. If you're arguing that A caused B, you need evidence beyond timing. Explain the mechanism. Rule out alternative explanations. Show that the relationship is not coincidental.

Slippery Slope (The Alarmist Fallacy)

'If we allow students to bring laptops to class, soon they'll be watching movies, then playing games, then the entire education system will collapse.' This is an exaggeration. One step does not inevitably lead to disaster.

If you're using a slippery slope, you need to show why each step is likely, not just possible. Explain the mechanism. Provide evidence. Don't rely on fear.

False Dilemma (Either/Or)

'We can either ban phones in school or accept that students will never learn.' This ignores the middle ground. Phones could be restricted, not banned. Phones could be used as learning tools. The world is more than two options.

If your argument presents only two choices, ask yourself: are there really only two? If not, revise.

Circular Reasoning (Begging the Question)

'The law is moral because it's against the law to be immoral.' This doesn't explain anything. It just restates the claim in different words.

Check your arguments for circularity. Are you assuming what you're trying to prove? If so, you need evidence from outside your claim.

How to Audit Your Own Essay

After you finish a draft, read it specifically for fallacies. One fallacy per read-through. First pass: look for straw men. Second pass: look for ad hominem. Third pass: look for false causes.

Fix each one. Then ask a friend to read it. Tell them to be ruthless. The goal is not to feel smart. The goal is to be correct. Fallacies feel good to write. They feel bad to have pointed out. But catching them yourself is better than having a professor catch them.
